sentences.text: Well, continuing the story about the peasant and the pig, the peasant and his wife were just crying, you know, don't take the pig.
Part of it was, in fact, they were very poor.
Part of it was that if the word would get out that they gave a pig, because everywhere in the village would know a pig is missing.
I mean, this is how pure the place is.
If they give to the partisans, you know, they would-- they could-- they could possibly be killed as a hostage.
And I take out my Walter nine millimeter.
I shoot the pig.
Carve it up, take half the pig.
He said, OK, you can have half the pig.
And I start dragging half the pig out.
You know, these were not big pigs.
These were little pigs.
And the guy's just crying.
So I-- I said, you know, oh pity.
Give me a piece of paper.
I'll give you a receipt for the pig.
And so I write-- by the way, by that time, I was Sergeant.
Sergeant Paul Strassmann, the Czechoslovak partisan, hereby certifies that-- that-- that so and so gave us a pig as a support of the National Liberation movement.
I said, how much is the pig worth?
He says, it's worth everything I got.
I said, put 50,000 crowns. 50,000 crowns, signed Paul Strassmann.
In 1945, in August, I walk down through the street of Trencin, again with my pistol in my pocket, because the times were still-- some scores were still being settled.
And I see this big hulking peasant run across the square directly at me.
And I say, well, God knows what this is.
And I just put my hand on my pistol.
The guy comes, kisses me.
He took that receipt, and he got a large German farm with that receipt as a reward for supporting the partisans.
The moral of the story is-- the moral of the story is you never know when you do something wrong that it may not turn out to be right.
OK.
Yeah, in '45--
In-- in February of 1945, we got marching orders to start moving towards the front.
The-- the-- the attack through the mountains turned out to be much more difficult than expected, and they wanted to use the partisan troops as a way of-- of-- of softening the rear of the advance.
The-- the-- the attack-- the Russian attack was really slowed down substantially in February of 1945.
In February of '45, I developed pleurisy, and I was bleeding from my lungs, actually.
And so I was set aside as a troop of people to march through the German lines east, to a hospital.
Because by that time, everybody knew the end of the war was coming.
The question was to salvage as much as possible.
So in March 25, marching nights over snow, mountains, we finally crossed the lines.
Lots of dead bodies.
The-- the woods were full of dead bodies, and some of them frost bitten, some of them out of starvation, some of them killed for all sorts of reasons.
Joined the Czechoslovak army, the official army.
Got sent to hospital where they fed me continually for four weeks.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And in late April of-- you don't have to pick up.
You don't have to pick up.
I'm not.
I'm just silencing.
Late in April 1945, I was sent to a special school.
The objective of that school was to send trusted members of the partisan to be a part of the military police to-- to do purges in the newly occupied territories.
I was in the school.
When the war actually ended, I was in the school.
Remember it the way it was today.
We went out, and we're shooting our guns, and God knows what.
Some stray bullets must have hit some civilians, because there was lots of wounded on that day in the hospital, but superficial wounds.
And then, I-- I just got marching orders to go to Bratislava, where my post would be and I would be assigned as an aide-de-camp to a colonel in the military police and support him.
So I took my Tommy gun.
And on my way to Bratislava, I stopped in Trencin, you know-- you know-- you know, what happened?
Where are my-- where-- you know.
So far as I knew, my mother could still be hidden, and my sister could still be hidden.
Well, there was no news from anybody.
I quickly found out that my mother and my sister were taken, and my grandparents were immediately deported.
And their chances of their survival was nil.
Did not know what my father was dead.
And stopped there for two days.
Of course, all the collaborators were sort of trembling.
You know, what will this kid do with a Tommy gun?
After strutting on the main square and-- for a while in my new uniform, I went to Bratislava, took on my post.
Stayed in the army for another four months.
Didn't like it.
The colonel was a very wonderful person.
Doctor Langer-- he was one of the chief defenders of the Communist party in trials, show trials, in the '30s-- said, look, kid.
And so, in the fall of 1945, I went back to-- to Trencin.
By that time, my sister had returned from concentration camp.
Sold some of the properties that my father had, because I didn't care about property.
Took money and went to Prague for two years, studying English, French, Spanish.
Going to theater every night.
Going to-- going to concert every afternoon.
You know, sort of filling-- filling myself up.
In December of 1947, I-- I got actually a teacher's certificate from the University, in English, so I could teach English.
By that time, I was absolutely convinced that-- that there's no place for me in Czechoslovakia.
Especially after seeing the criminal element from the underground rise to the top in Slovakia.
Much of the so-called partisan movement was criminal, and they are the ones who took some of the key positions.

In fact, the man with whom I shared fleas and lice in some of the hay rakes that we slept together, Staudinger, became the chief prosecuting judge in show trials, including the hanging of the former president of Slovakia.
And in my opinion, he was just a criminal.
So by that time, Mr.
Kubicek, who had left Slovakia in 1939 for Basel and left his wife and his child in the care of my father, offered to take me to America.
Which, of course, was what everybody wanted to do.
Couldn't get me a visa, an immigration visa.
So-- but it was quite becoming apparent the Communists would be taking over.
And so, five days before the Communists took over in February of 1948, I just walked out of Czechoslovakia on a train with a short term visa for England under the pretense that I'm going to school.
Electronic school, to study electronic engineering in England.
Went to England.
The brother of Mrs.
Kubicek by that time was well established in England.
He supported me.
You know, I went to school, catching up on my education.
Got excellent private education in England in-- from-- from February '48 to October '48.
And in October 15, '48, I got finally an American immigration visa, which was very hard to get.
Got on a ship, a freighter ship, in Plymouth, England for $95.
I got a berth on a freighter ship.
And 13 days later, showed up in America.
For the first 18 month, I stayed with the Kubicek family in their house.
My first job was to-- to sell socks at the Gertz department store in Jamaica, New York for $0.65 an hour, which I thought was absolutely fantastic income.
You know, you could really live in America for $0.65 an hour in those days, I mean, luxuriously.
You know, by my standards.
I was told that-- that in order to be able to go to university, I need a high school diploma.
So while I was selling socks, I also went at night to Jamaica High School for a year and got a high school diploma.
Graduated from the high school in May of 1949.
I applied for admission to City College, Brooklyn College, and this place called Cooper Union that everybody told me I could not possibly get into because it's so competitive, and it's free.
Whereas in Brooklyn College, at City College, you had to pay for lab fees.
It was free, no tuition, but lab fees.
And there was no way in the world how I was going to get $150 somehow.
I mean, just, you know, the idea of having $150 was just beyond my imagination.
I applied to Cooper Union.
The admissions officer of Cooper Union sent me a letter in September of 1949 saying, we regret we cannot admit you to college.
You just about made it, but-- but-- but we are going to put you on a waiting list.
You have a very unusual score.

See, in those days, the examinations were an SAT, you know, free choices, and I was not used-- see culturally, the way you take examinations in America is totally different from the kind of education I had, which was classical, where you had a discussion.
He said, you had the highest score I've seen in a long time in geometry and mathematics.
You totally flunked English.
I said, well sir, you know my literary capabilities are rather limited.
About 15th of September, 1949, I get a letter from Cooper Union saying, well, somebody who was offered a job-- a seat in Cooper Union decided to go to MIT.
We have a seat available in the civil engineering department.
We need an answer today.
I said, that's it.
So I went to Cooper Union and enrolled late in September of 1949.
Was elected class president in my junior year.
I was the chairman of the judiciary.
Cooper Union was one of the earliest colleges with self governing, and it had-- the students did disciplinary action.
I was the chairman of the judiciary.
Supported myself right throughout school.
Worked Saturdays and summers on civil engineering jobs.
Generated-- graduated summa cum laude, Tau Beta Pi.
And met this girl at Cooper Union.
When I graduated with an engineering degree, I concluded that I did not have a good education.
So in those days, Cooper Union graduate by the name of Rossi offered a scholarship for promising Cooper Union students to go to MIT Sloan School for a two year master's program.
I applied for the scholarship.
And before I handed my application, the admissions officer said, you got it.
You don't have to apply.
Because Rossi specially provided for-- for a scholarship for people of European background who are promising for America.
And I said, well, that's fine, but Strassmanns don't take charity from anybody, and I will not accept your scholarship.
So if Rossi wants to get me into Sloan School, I'll go and pay my own tuition.
But I will not accept a-- a grant.
This is part of the Strassmann heritage, you know.
We never take anything from anybody.
It's just a matter of principle.
My wife has had-- really held this against me for the rest of her life.
Everybody else considered that that was just totally outright stupid.
The other thing, the reason why I left the Kubiceks, of course, is the Kubiceks were part of the refugee community, talking about refugee matters, talking Slovak.
Really being alienated in the same way as the Borochov pyramid of my youth, told me these people were getting alienated.
And I surely did not want that.
By the way, in summer of 1949, after completing surveying camp, I went to Israel on behalf of the Jewish agency.
Surveyed lands, Arab lands, near Latrun to divide it for-- for settlers.
Got shot at, which was OK by me.
They were very poor shots there, anyway.
And so graduated from MIT.
While at MIT, I told you I was working part time in civil engineering.
And I was going to be a civil engineer.
I worked for a consulting firm.
They built the New Jersey Turnpike.
In one of the sweltering summers in 1953, after graduation, in the drafting room, the partner of the consulting firm, Elmer Timby walks by and says, I understand you're going to MIT.
Do you know any statistics?
I said, of course I know statistics.
He says, well, we have a problem.
We have a dispute on the New Jersey Turnpike.
The toll collectors are going to go on strike, because they think they're overworked.

The government thinks-- the governor thinks that the toll collectors are really featherbedding themselves, because whenever the governor goes to work, there are these guys just sitting at the toll booth doing nothing.
Of course, the governor goes to work at 11 o'clock in the morning.
Obviously, he has a different view than somebody during rush hour.

The consulting firm who built-- the engineering firm who built the New Jersey Turnpike, a construction firm, Howard, Needles, Tammen, and Bergendoff, got the project assignment to figure out whether the toll collectors were overworked or underworked.

And so since I was the only guy in the drafting room who claimed that he knew anything about statistics-- which was, as a matter of fact, correct-- got the job of figuring out the overworking of the toll collection personnel.

Turned out that in those days, the New Jersey Turnpike was the only place in the world other than the Bureau of Census that had punch cards which showed when vehicles came on the turnpike and when they got off.
So I just said, well send me two months' worth of toll cards for the Jersey Turnpike.
A truck arrived, and I said, how do I get through these cards?
By this time I'm at MIT, and somebody tells me, you know, there's this peculiar machine in the laboratory called a computer, you know.
That-- that could sort of analyze these cards.

To make the story short, I wrote the first dissertation in the United States on business applications of computers, which is still considered to be somewhat of a classical, 'The Scheduling of Toll Collection Personnel on the New Jersey Turnpike.' Got paid royally for that thing.
Got paid royally for it.
Could afford getting married.
You know, I was drawing $4,000 a year, by that time, going to school, graduate school.
Paid for tuition, got married, could afford $35 a month rent in Marine barracks from-- left from Second World War.
I mean, pig's heaven.
[LAUGHTER]
And got into computers.
The year was 1954?
And stayed in computers ever since.
That's a great-- great story, a great tidal wave that took you through a remarkable career.
And-- and you-- you spent part of your career in recent years doing work for the US government.
Well--
Oh, well that-- you know, you always find these philosophical explanations after the fact.
I-- I think people are what they are.
They have certain beliefs, a certain consistency.
I mean, it's amazing that I meet my peer group who are my age now, in Israel.
In-- in terms of character and attitude, they fundamentally are still the same as they were 50 years ago when I knew them.
I'm basically a creature of my environment.
And-- and after being successful in business and being vice president and chief computer executive and a rather prominent member of the computer fraternity, held a number of very significant posts.
When I retired from Xerox, I started doing work in my specialty, leading to-- to-- to an assignment for General Motors to arbitrate a very major dispute of General Motors.
I always did public service, by the way, throughout my whole career.
I always was on government advisory commissions.
In fact, I was offered a number of government posts from time to time, which I couldn't afford with a family of four.
But I would not say that I went to the government because of some grand plan.
I mean, I never would have thought that I would be offered a senior government post.

But it just happened that I solved the problem for General Motors, and then the vice chairman of General Motors became Deputy Secretary of Defense and was-- was given the job to cut $72 billion dollars, largely through introduction of automation.
And he said, I have just the man who can do that.
And so I was offered a job down in Washington.
And I'm just a good soldier, you know.
I just-- you know, it's like in the partisans.
You know, the guys ask me, well, go and walk in the front.
So I'd walk in the front.
So Atwood asked me walk in the front over really taking the Department of Defense apart.
So I said, fine, I'll do it.
And-- and I did it.
And-- and I'm very proud of that.
It was a wonderful, glorious association which I'm continuing with the Department of Defense.
There is a-- somehow, I clicked very, very, very well with the military.
And-- and they-- they consider me as one of the people who-- who they can trust.
And when the Clinton administration came in, they immediately found a spot, a senior spot for me at West Point, as a professor at West Point.
Which sort of doesn't happen to young refugees from a small town in Slovakia too often.
I will play a song which is a memorial song for a fallen [INAUDIBLE].
It is really stolen from a Russian 1905 revolutionary song.
But as I mentioned to you before, Eli [INAUDIBLE] was heavily influenced by Russian roots and Russian origin, so this is not altogether strange.
[PLAYING ACCORDION]
You want me to play-- [PLAYING ACCORDION]
